title: Mobius Harness
description: Use when a software delivery request spans requirements, planning, implementation, validation, PR/MR handling, CI/CD tracking, or delivery reporting.

input_schema.request: string — user's feature, bugfix, refactor, or delivery goal
input_schema.repo_path: string — local repository path, usually the current working directory
input_schema.constraints: string — optional scope, timeline, compatibility, testing, release, or governance constraints
input_schema.run_id: string — optional kebab-case id for persisted delivery artifacts

instructions: Use Mobius Harness as the primary entrypoint for end-to-end delivery work with one accountable agent unless delegation is explicitly requested.

Use the references directory progressively: delivery-process.md for gates and status, artifact-interface.md for artifact rules, hook-policy.md for Claude Code/Codex hook controls and soft or hard agent gates, artifact-templates.md for canonical file templates, and governance-and-reporting.md for completion, safety, PR/MR, release, and resume rules.
Select Lightweight, Standard, or Strict mode at the start; use persisted .delivery/runs/<run-id>/ artifacts for Standard and Strict work.

For Standard and Strict deliveries, initialize missing persisted artifacts and harness-owned hook gates with bash scripts/init-delivery-run.sh <run-id> --request "<user request>" [--gate-type soft|hard] [--runtime auto|codex|claude-code|claude|generic] when that script exists; default initialization to soft gates and runtime auto-detection unless user, repository policy, release risk, security risk, merge policy, or executor evidence semantics require explicit overrides; normalize claude to claude-code in generated artifacts; treat initialized artifacts and hook scripts as a blocked starting state, not final validation.
Follow the delivery process in order and treat phase gates as blocking controls; do not advance until the current gate is pass, not-applicable, or an explicitly recorded exception.

For Standard and Strict deliveries, maintain Hook Ledger rows for required hooks from hook-policy.md; classify Required Action values with [hard] or [soft], treat blocked hooks as blocking controls, and allow warn only for soft gates with mirrored Failure List and Change List records.

Maintain Review Ledger rows for required adversarial reviews from delivery-process.md and treat blocked reviews as blocking controls before publishing phase results or starting the next execution phase.
Split large, risky, or blocked phases into subphases, and keep a status record for every phase and subphase.
Each phase or subphase status record must include Goal, Checklist, Gate Ledger, Hook Ledger for Standard and Strict deliveries, Review Ledger, Todo List, Failure List, and Change List.
Maintain Gate Ledger rows with gate id, phase, required evidence, status, evidence pointer, and exception detail when relevant.
Maintain Hook Ledger rows with hook id, trigger, required action prefixed by [hard] or [soft], status, evidence pointer, and failure handling when relevant.

Maintain Review Ledger rows with review id, role, perspective, challenge, status, resolution, and evidence; use distinct product, engineering, architecture, validation, security, operations, or user perspectives as appropriate.
Analyze requirements before implementation by clarifying the goal, background, success criteria, scope, non-goals, risks, open questions, and the superpowers:brainstorming decision.

When a request references an issue, bug report, PR, branch, fork, commit, or previous fix, record Issue and Prior Attempts with evidence from issue comments, linked PRs, related PR search, fork commits, or an explicit not-applicable reason.
Treat prior attempts as design evidence rather than copy instructions: identify reusable tests or ideas, stale assumptions, behavioral differences, and fresh verification required before adoption.

Classify requirements uncertainties as blocking, accepted, deferred, or not-applicable; do not advance to design until Requirements Maturity is ready-for-design or an accepted-risk exception is recorded.

Record Minimum Skill Dependencies during requirements and carry them into planning, including mobius-harness, local-repo-development, superpowers:brainstorming, and superpowers:writing-plans with evidence and fallback handling.
Use superpowers:brainstorming before creative work, feature shaping, behavior design, unclear product intent, or competing solution paths; otherwise record why it is not applicable.

Inspect the repository, identify relevant specialist skills, carry forward Minimum Skill Dependencies, compare discovered prior attempts against the selected approach, and produce a delivery plan with implementation steps, validation commands, Validation Prerequisites, acceptance criteria, checkpoints, Dependency Decision, and the superpowers:writing-plans decision.
Compare design options and rejected alternatives before implementation; do not start coding until Design Readiness is ready-for-implementation or an accepted-risk exception is recorded.
When prior attempts depend on time-sensitive APIs, package behavior, platform capabilities, or external services, record Freshness Evidence before using or rejecting their approach.
Use superpowers:writing-plans for Standard or Strict deliveries, risky work, multi-step implementation, or agent handoff plans; otherwise record why it is not applicable.
When Superpowers skills are used, record spec or plan artifact paths; when unavailable, record fallback handling as blocked, not-applicable, or exception.
Record Dependency Decision as no-new-dependency, existing-toolchain, or new-dependency-required, with reason, evidence, and fallback before implementation.
Use the local-repo-development workflow for git worktree setup, branch selection, preservation of unrelated user changes, commit preparation, and CI/CD tracking.

Implement the smallest coherent change, run relevant local validation, review the diff for requirements compliance, implementation quality, test adequacy, security, and regressions before committing, and scan changed files for sensitive information without printing secret values.

Create a PR or MR when requested or required, then default to asynchronous CI/CD follow-up during small iterative updates by recording head SHA, check URLs, and next observation point without blocking the user.

Wait synchronously for CI/CD only when the user requests full waiting, when merging, when releasing, when repository policy requires terminal checks, or when an observed failure has been fixed and the user chooses to wait for the next run.
Produce a delivery report covering requirements, implementation, changed files, local validation, code review, sensitive information scan, PR or MR URL, CI/CD status, risks, and follow-ups.

For long or risky tasks, maintain .delivery/runs/<run-id>/requirements.md, plan.md, verification.md, delivery-report.md, generated .delivery/hooks/config.json, .delivery/hooks/agent_gate.sh, executable .delivery/hooks/<hook-id>.sh gates, and runtime project settings under .claude/settings.json or .codex/settings.json when initialized; prefer repository initialization scripts over hand-written initial artifacts when available; for short tasks, include the same facts and a compact Gate Ledger in the final response.

For Standard and Strict deliveries, run bash scripts/validate-delivery-run.sh .delivery/runs/<run-id> before marking delivery complete when the script exists; record failure or unavailability as a gate or hook result.
Do not mark a phase, subphase, or delivery complete without evidence such as commands, file paths, diffs, PR/MR URLs, CI/CD URLs, user decisions, or an explicit reason evidence is unavailable.
Do not mark a phase, subphase, or delivery complete while any related Gate Ledger row is blocked.
Do not mark a phase, subphase, or delivery complete while any required Hook Ledger or Review Ledger row is missing or blocked, or while a hard Hook Ledger row is downgraded to warn.

Each persisted artifact belongs to the Delivery Episode Package and must include status, phase marker or timestamp, evidence, and enough phase/subphase state for another agent to audit and resume the delivery.
When blocked, record the failure, try one safe minimal recovery, and ask the user only for the specific decision needed.

When resuming, read the Delivery Episode Package first, identify the earliest incomplete phase or subphase, review Todo List, Failure List, and Change List, confirm git state, and continue from the first unmet gate.

A delivery is complete only when requirements, Issue and Prior Attempts when applicable, Requirements Maturity, Prior Attempt Comparison when applicable, Design Readiness, Minimum Skill Dependencies, Validation Prerequisites, Superpowers planning decisions, Dependency Decision, required hard and soft hook decisions, required adversarial review decisions, implementation scope, changed files, validation, diff review, sensitive information scan, PR/MR state, CI/CD state, residual risks, release notes, and follow-ups are all reported.

examples.input: Add a new API endpoint, open a PR, and make sure CI passes.
examples.output: Clarify endpoint behavior and compatibility, search linked issues and prior PR attempts when available, compare reusable ideas and stale assumptions, apply API review and test generation, complete adversarial Review Ledger checks, create or reuse a safe worktree, implement the endpoint, run local validation, review the diff, scan for sensitive information, commit, open a PR, record CI/CD head SHA and check URLs asynchronously by default, and return a delivery report with the PR URL, validation status, CI/CD observation state, and any follow-up trigger for full waiting.
